steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence introduces a person by job title followed directly by their name. Determine whether any punctuation is needed between the title and the name.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'economist': No punctuation between the title and the proper name — correct for a restrictive appositive where the name identifies which economist is meant. ✓
  • Choice B — 'economist—': An em dash signals an abrupt break or interruption; nothing after the name justifies returning from a dash, so this is structurally incorrect. ✗
  • Choice C — 'economist:': A colon introduces a list or explanation; 'Arthur Laffer' is not an explanation of 'economist,' so the colon is unwarranted. ✗
  • Choice D — 'economist,': A comma here would create a non-restrictive appositive implying only one economist exists, which is not the intended meaning and adds unnecessary punctuation. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
